coredata.dc:title: A novel metal organic gel with superior oxidase-like activity for efficient and sensitive chemiluminescence detection of uric acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Mengjuan
Wei, Wei
Wang, Weifeng
Chen, Hongli
He, Jiang
coredata.dc:description: It is found that MIL-100(Fe) gels, as a kind of metal-organic gels (MOGs), constitutting of iron (Fe3+) and trimesic acid (H3BTC), has been regarded as the efficient catalyst of luminol chemiluminescence (CL) system without the presence of extra oxidants in the present work. MIL-100(Fe) gels that have possessed mimicking oxidase-like activity can excellently enhanced luminol CL intensity by accelerating the generation of reactive oxygen species. Furthermore, with the addition of uric acid (UA), the CL signal has been dramatically inhibited under alkaline condition. Hence, the CL intensity inhibiting ratio (I 0/I S) was proportional to the increasing concentration of UA in the rang from 10 nM to 4000 nM with the detection limit of 5.9 nM. This method has been successfully applied for analysis of UA with acceptable recoveries ranging from 97.0% to 107.9% in urine sample. These results indicates that this study open up a novel, sensitive and convenient method to detect UA in biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MIL-100(Fe) gels
Chemiluminescence
Oxidase-like catalytic activity
Uric acid

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of flow injection-chemiluminescence (FI-CL) system studies
The activity of MIL-100(Fe) gel as catalyst for luminol CL system
Determination of UA in human urine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SEM (a), (b) and TEM (c), (d) images of MIL-100 (Fe) gel before CL reaction.
XPS spectrum of Fe-Fe-MOGs (a) and Fe 2p (b), FT-IR spectra of MIL-100(Fe) gel (c).
CL spectra of luminol, luminol-Fe3+, luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel and luminol-MIL-100(Fe) (with equivalent Fe3+content).
UV–vis absorption spectra of luminol (black line), MIL-100(Fe) gel (red line), and luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel (blue line) under the optimized conditions.
CL response curves (a) of luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel system under oxygen-saturated (black line), air-saturated (red line) and nitrogen-saturated solution (blue line), respectively.
(a) CL emission-time curves in different concentrations of UA,(b) Linear calibration curve of UA in the range of 10 nM-4000 nM.
Schematic diagram to show the luminol CL system for the detection of UA with MIL-100(Fe) gel as a catalyst.

Schematic diagram of FI-CL system. (a) Alkaline solution of luminol, (b) Na2CO3-NaHCO3 buffer solution, (c) MIL-100(Fe) gel solution and (d) serotonin or sample solution. P: peristaltic pump; PMT: photomultiplier; V: injection valve; W: waste solution.
Schematic diagram of the emission mechanism of luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel CL system.
Comparison of developed analytical methods for the determination of UA.
Specificity of the proposed work toward UA.
Determination of UA in biological samples.

head.ce:title: A novel metal organic gel with superior oxidase-like activity for efficient and sensitive chemiluminescence detection of uric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is found that MIL-100(Fe) gels, as a kind of metal-organic gels (MOGs), constitutting of iron (Fe3+) and trimesic acid (H3BTC), has been regarded as the efficient catalyst of luminol chemiluminescence (CL) system without the presence of extra oxidants in the present work. MIL-100(Fe) gels that have possessed mimicking oxidase-like activity can excellently enhanced luminol CL intensity by accelerating the generation of reactive oxygen species. Furthermore, with the addition of uric acid (UA), the CL signal has been dramatically inhibited under alkaline condition. Hence, the CL intensity inhibiting ratio (I 0/I S) was proportional to the increasing concentration of UA in the rang from 10 nM to 4000 nM with the detection limit of 5.9 nM. This method has been successfully applied for analysis of UA with acceptable recoveries ranging from 97.0% to 107.9% in urine sample. These results indicates that this study open up a novel, sensitive and convenient method to detect UA in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (2,6,8-trihydroxypurine, UA), which is the principal end product of purine (e.g., adenine, guanine) derivatives, is generated from the metabolization of the human bodies [1,2]. It is momentous marker molecule and extensively exists in serum and urine. Generally, the normal content of uric acid in healthy adults is in the rang of 1.49 × 10−3 M to 4.46 × 10−3 M in urine [3]. Abnormal uric acid levels can lead to a serious of severe diseases. Excessive uric acid level could be lead to hyperuricemia, urathritis, arthrophlogosis, cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases, etc [4–6]. Low uric acid level might associated with multiple sclerosis, pernicious anemia [7–8]. Therefore, establishing an appropriate method for acquiring uric acid content in human urine has been attached with great importance to the human health.

Detection of UA levels is mostly involved in various analytical methods, including electrochemistry analysis [9–12], colorimetric methods [13,14], voltammetric method [15], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16], fluorescent analysis [17], chemiluminescence (CL) [18,19], and so on. Among these methods, CL is an easy-to-operate, rapid, efficient, low-cost analytic method to detect the content of UA in biologic samples. And then, flow injection analysis (FIA) has the simple and convenient operation, small amount of reagents consumed, etc. Thus, flow injection-chemiluminescence (FI-CL) as the fast, efficient and highly sensitive analytical technique, has been used widely by researchers due to its fast, efficient, simple and highly sensitive analytical activities [20]. In recent years, most of researchers have been attracted much attention to luminol FI-CL system.

However, the most of luminol CL systems are unstable due to relying on an oxidant, such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [21], NaIO4 [22], KMnO4 [23]. Therefore, it has been essential for developing luminol CL system without extra oxidants to detect UA content. Nowadays, many kinds of luminol CL systems were improved by supplying catalytic materials, including metallic oxide [24], metal nanoparticles [25,26], carbon dots [27], metal organic frameworks (MOF) [28,29], silicon quantum dots [30], liquid-layered double hydroxide (LDH) [31], metal organic gels (MOGs) [32,33], and so on. Among these materials, MOGs with a poor crystalline morphology nanomaterial, which were synthesized by assembling organic ligands and metal ions through metal-ligand coordination and noncovalent interactions with a simple, fast and facile hydrothermal method, have an excellent oxidase-like catalytic activity in the process of transforming dissolved oxygen to reactive oxide species (ROS) [32].

With this in mind, a novel, fast, remarkable sensitive method, which was the luminol CL system without extra oxidants in the presence of MIL-100(Fe) gel, were established to detect the content of UA in human urine. In the present study (Scheme 1 ), MIL-100(Fe) gel, as an oxidase-like catalyst, can cause strong CL by transforming dissolved oxygen to ROS reacted with alkaline luminol solution. The obvious CL signal of luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel system was efficiently quenched in the presence of UA.

Methanol, tert-butylalcohol, ethanol and isopropanol were supplied from Li'anlong Bohua Medicine Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium carbonate decahydrate, iron(III) chloride hexahydrate and sodium bicarbonate were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Superoxide dismutase (SOD), ascorbic acid (AA), glutamic acid (Glu), glucose, and creatinine, serine, tyrosine, uric acid (UA), L-cysteine (Cys), and L-tryptophan (Trp) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Trimesic acid (H3BTC) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Luminol (3-aminophthalhydrazide) was supplied from Nanjing Odofuni Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Urea, p-Quinone, thiourea, and other reagents were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All of the reagents were analytically pure and used without extra purification. The ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was detected by NEXUS 670 (Nicolet, USA). A RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to investigate Fluorometric measurement. Quantitative analysis of elements was obtained by a plasma quant PQ9000 inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) (Analytik Jena, German). Scanning spectrum was carried out by a TU-1901 ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Purkine General, China). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were obtained on an X’Pert PRO diffractometer (Panalytical, Netherlands). The transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi-600, Japan) and the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, Japan) were used to characterize the morphology of mil-100(Fe) gel. The CL signals was collected by a IFFM-E flow injection CL analyzer (Remex, China).
The synthetic procedures of MIL-100(Fe) were adopted from previous reports [34].

In brief, H3BTC (0.52 g, 0.14 M) and FeCl3·6H2O (1.02 g, 0.21 M) were added to 18 mL of ethanol for 15 min under stirring at room temperature. Then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave container, which was sealed in a stainless steel vessel. After heating at 120 °C for 1 h, a brown transparent metal-organic gel was acquired and the solvent was removed in the obtained wet gel by a drying oven. And then the xerogel particles were obtained.
MIL-100(Fe) was synthesized according to the previous reported work [35], and the detailed processes were as followings.

H3BTC (416.1 mg, 0.66 mM) and FeCl3·6H2O (486.6 mg, 1 mM) were dispersed in 15 mL of ultrapure water. After stirred at room temperature for 10 min, the mixture was heated at 130 °C for 72 h in a Teflon container, which was sealed in a stainless steel vessel. After cooling to the room temperature, the yellow solid was collected by centrifugation. To remove the unreacted reagent, the product was purified by washing using ultrapure water and ethanol at 70 °C for 4 h. After centrifugation, MIL-100(Fe) was eventually obtained by drying overnight at 130 °C under vacuum conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of flow injection-chemiluminescence (FI-CL) system studies
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 2 , it was presented the diagram of the FI-CL system for detecting UA. The procedure of flow injection-chemiluminescence (FI-CL) system was based on previous works [36,37]. The solution of all channels were pumped by two peristaltic pumps at the rate of 2.8 mL/min. Firstly, the alkaline solution of luminol (a) and Na2CO3-NaHCO3 buffer solution (b) were blended and propelled into the flow cell. MIL-100(Fe) gel solution (c) and the blank or UA sample solution (d) were continuously mixed and propelled into the the sampling loop (800 μL) by two peristaltic pumps. And then, the mixture of solutions was pumped into the flow cell. Eventually, the CL data collection was investigated by a computer. The PMT voltage of 650 V was applied for the CL detection of UA.

Luminol stock solution (1.0 mM, 0.018 g), which made by dissolving luminol in 100 mL NaOH solution (10.0 mM, 0.04 g), was stored in the dark at 4 oC for at least a week before use to make the luminol stock solution stable [31]. Carbonate buffer solution (pH 9.2) were prepared by adjusting sodium carbonate decahydrate (0.1 M) and sodium bicarbonate (0.1 M) that preparated of solution. MIL-100(Fe) gel solution (0.025 g/L, 0.005 g) were prepared by dissolving metal-organic gel in 250 mL of aqueous solution and stored in the refrigerator (4 °C) before uses.

The urine samples were collected from healthy volunteer of laboratory to better validate the specificity and applicability of our present method. The urine samples have been diluted 10,000-fold by current carrier step by step without any pretreatment.

Morphology of MIL-100(Fe) gel was provided with SEM (Fig. 1 (a), 1(b)) and TEM (Fig. 1(c), 1(d)) images of MIL-100(Fe) gel, which revealed that MIL-100(Fe) gel was platelet-like structure. As depicted in Figure S1, the XRD pattern of MIL-100(Fe) gel was in agreement with the reported work [34], which indicates the successful formation of MIL-100(Fe) gel without good crystalline structure. On the contrary, MIL-100(Fe) has a excellent crystalline structure.

XPS pattern of Fe-MOGs displyed in Fig. 2 (a) that C1s (284.2), O1s (530.1), Fe2s (710.6,724.9), Cl2s (266.1), Cl3s (196.9), Cl2p (55.3) were co-existed in the sample [33,34]. To clearly obtain the Fe component, the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 2(b)) appeared the two typical peaks at 710.6 eV and 724.9 eV attributed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. These results indicated that the coordination of Fe3+ and H3BTC [32]. Fig. 2(b) showed As clearly shown in Fig. 2(c), the characterization of MIL-100(Fe) gel were depicted by the FT-IR spectra. The absorption peaks at 482 cm−1 and 757 cm−1 were matched with FeO bonds and stretching vibration of CC bands of benzene ring, respectively [38]. The adsorption peaks, which were observed at 1383 cm−1, 1447 cm−1, 1582 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1, were assignable to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of carboxyl groups. The results of Δν(νas(COO)-νs(COO)) are 180 cm−1 and 199 cm−1, indicating the generation coordination mode between the center of metal ion and carboxyl group [39,40]. Furthermore, the wide absorption peak at 3450 cm−1 was potentially correspond to HO vibration of coordinated OH or adsorbed H2O [41]. The results above indicated that the successful synthesis of MIL-100(Fe) gel in a simple and mild method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The activity of MIL-100(Fe) gel as catalyst for luminol CL system
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the activity of MIL-100(Fe) gel as oxidase-like catalyst for luminol CL system, the kinetic curves of luminol, MIL-100(Fe) gel and luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel CL systems were performed under the optimized conditions (shown in Figure S2, pH 9.2, 0.02 mM luminol dissolved in 0.001 M NaOH, 0.025 mg/mL MIL-100(Fe) gel). The ratio of iron elements was 35.88% and 20.34% in MIL-100(Fe) gel and MIL-100(Fe) obtained by ICP-OES, respectively. As Fig. 3 illustrated, there was no obvious CL emission could be observed with the luminol alkaline solution pumped into the cell (black line). However, under the same experimental conditions, it was observed a stronger CL signal with the injection of MIL-100(Fe) gel solution (green line), as compared to the weaker CL intensity in the presence of Fe3+ solution (red line) and MIL-100(Fe) solution (blue line), respectively. The C increased about about 50-fold after injecting MIL-100(Fe) gel solution compared to the weak CL intensity with adding only luminol system. These system’s comparisons of CL intensity implys that Fe3+ in the MIL-100(Fe) gel might paly an crucial role, while indicates that the excellent activity catalytic of MIL-100(Fe) gel for luminol CL in alkaline solution due to it could exhibit a large amount of exposed activity sites in the surface of MIL-100(Fe) gel, which is platelet-like structure.

In order to verify the CL enhancing phenomena of MIL-100(Fe) gel in this luminol system, the following experiments were proceeding. The role of MIL-100(Fe) gel in this system were investigated by UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 4 ), there was two absorption brands of luminol at 300 nm and 350 nm [32]. At the same time, it exhibited that the absorption wavelengths had no significant difference with the absence and presence of MIL-100(Fe) gel, indicating that there was not generated new species with the addition of MIL-100(Fe) gel, further demonstrating that the enhanced CL intensity of luminol system should be caused to MIL-100(Fe) gel with the catalytic activity.

To further investigate the role of MIL-100(Fe) gel as catalyst in luminol system, as seen in Fig. 3S, there was a a single peak of this luminol system centered at ~440 nm with the presence of MIL-100(Fe) gel [42,43], indicating that the luminophor was the excited 3-anminophthalate anions (3-APA*), which was agreement with previous reported work [42].

Furthermore, the traditional luminol CL system generally participated in the presence of oxidants according to the previous studies [44]. In this work, it could be observed the strong luminol CL intensity without adding any extra oxidants in alkaline solution for the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [41,42,45,46]. The following experiments were explored to further verify the CL mechanism. The O2-saturated and N2-saturated reactant solutions were bubbled with O2, N2 for 1 h, respectively, while the air-saturated solution was not bubbled. As seen the Fig. 5 , it described the differences of the intensity of luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel system with the presence nitrogen gas, air and oxygen gas, which indicated that dissolved oxygen play a momentous role on the generation ROS in this CL system.

To thoroughly disclose the mechanism, different radicals which were singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radical (
OH) and superoxide radical anion (
O2 −) were investigated with the help of ROS scavengers. As shown in Table S1, the CL emission of luminol system greatly inhibited with increasing the concentration of AA that was a proverbial radical scavenger, which indicated that ROS were generated in luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel CL system [47]. In order to further conform the kind of ROS, a series of radical scavenger were added the CL system. Primarily, the CL intensity were effectively inhibited with the addition of SOD or benzoquinone, as scavengers of
O2 −, indicating that
O2 − played an crucial role in this system [48]. Meanwhile, the CL signal were strongly quenched with the presence of isopropanol, tert-butylalcohol, methanol or thiourea, which were the scavengers of
OH, implying the generation of
OH in the process of CL reaction [49]. Furthermore, with the addition of sodium azide, as the scavenger of 1O2, the CL emission were stronger compared with the
OH,
O2 − scavengers, which showed that a small amount 1O2 took part in the CL reaction [50]. These results strongly support that
OH,
O2 − and 1O2 were the significant intermediates of luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel CL system.

Based on the above mentioned results, the mechanism of luminol CL reaction could be surmised in Scheme 3 . Above all, under alkaline conditions, dissolved oxygen were absorbed on the surface of MIL-100(Fe) gel that were catalyzed the generation of ROS [47], including
O2 −,
OH, and 1O2 (1). The oxidizing reaction between ROS and luminol has been produced the unstable excited-state 3-aminophthalate (3-APA*), which was consistent with the previous work [51–54]. At the same time, the strong CL signal has been observed when it returned to the ground-state (2) [55–58].

In order to investigate the practicability of this novel method better, the quenching efficiency of CL signals with UA under the optimum condition, which should be caused oxidation-reduction reaction both UA and ROS. As Fig. 6 illustrated, the CL emissions were quenched with the increasing concentration of UA, and the quenched efficiency (I 0/I), which the equation was I 0/I S = 0.0011 [UA] + 0.9595 (R 2 = 0.9994), was linear to the concentration of UA from 10 nM to 4000 nM with the detection limit of 5.9 nM. These results indicates that this proposed work put forward a high sensitive and convenient method to detect UA in biological samples. By comparison, the present work has wider detection rang and lower detection limit than other previous works (Table 1 ).

To better validate the specificity and selectivity of our developed method, the interference of some common interferences in urine sample was evaluated with the presence of 0.1 μM UA [67,68]. As seen in Table 2 , it showed that commonly co-existence of component did not interference with the determination of UA, which showed that luminol-MIL-100(Fe) gel CL system was evaluated with the excellent selectivity in the determination of UA. In addition, the similar CL signal detected in Figure S4, it showed that this method has excellent stability under optimized conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of UA in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the feasibility of the sensing system, the concentrations of UA in the diluted healthy human urine samples were tested. As shown in Table 3 , these results showed that the found mount were 1.78 × 10−3 M and 3.3 × 10−3 M in real samples conform to the normal content of uric acid in healthy adults [69,70]. Furthermore, this method have obtained corresponding recoveries ranging from 97.0% to 107.9% in human urine. Herein, the CL method could be appropriated for rapid detection of UA in real samples.

In conclusion, a novel MIL-100(Fe) gel , which performed an excellent catalytic activity in luminol CL system without extra oxidants, have been synthesized by a simple, fast and facile hydrothermal method. In this system, MIL-100(Fe) gel can cause strong CL intensity by transforming dissolved oxygen to ROS reacted with alkaline luminol solution. In addition, the obvious CL signal of this system was efficiently quenched by UA with wide detection rang and low detection limit. The well performance in real samples was gained. The presented work not only enrich the kinds of catalysts in the traditional luminol CL, but also broaden the potential applications of MIL-100(Fe) gel in CL field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119773.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
